authorName: American Airlines tweaks bag fees: Why you'll definitely want to plan ahead
content: American Airlines is raising the fee for a second checked bag to $50 at the airport, following a trend of increased bag fees in the airline industry and after reporting a significant drop in profits for 2025.

authorName: American Airlines tweaks bag fees: Why you'll definitely want to plan ahead
content: American Airlines has enhanced its mobile app and online tools to facilitate easier baggage addition and flight changes for customers.

authorName: American Airlines tweaks bag fees: Why you'll definitely want to plan ahead
content: American Airlines aims to encourage travelers to prepay for their second checked bag to reduce check-in and bag-drop lines at the airport.

authorName: American Airlines tweaks bag fees: Why you'll definitely want to plan ahead
content: American Airlines will charge $35 for the first checked bag if prepaid online and $40 if paid at the airport, with no changes to these fees.

authorName: American Airlines tweaks bag fees: Why you'll definitely want to plan ahead
content: American Airlines announced an increase in fees for passengers' second checked bag to $50 if paid at the airport, up from $45, while maintaining a $45 fee for those who prepay online.

authorName: Review: Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo Business Class
content: Matthew Klint believes that the Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo offers superior business class comfort compared to the A350-900, primarily due to the quality of the Collins Aerospace Elevation seats.

authorName: Review: Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo Business Class
content: Service on Malaysia Airlines' flight was warm and attentive, highlighted by a friendly purser named Mr. Teck Hui, who engaged in a pleasant conversation about his extensive experience in the airline industry.

authorName: Review: Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo Business Class
content: Malaysia Airlines served breakfast shortly after takeoff, offering options such as Nasi Lemak and Roasted Chicken Thigh, while Matthew Klint expressed disappointment over the inclusion of pungent 'petai' beans.

authorName: Review: Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo Business Class
content: Each business class seat on the Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo features a 17.3-inch 4K touch screen display with a decent selection of content, but the in-flight entertainment system is considered below average compared to competitors.

authorName: Review: Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo Business Class
content: Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo business class features 28 Collins Aerospace Elevation seats arranged in a 1-2-1 reverse herringbone pattern, with each seat offering 44 inches of pitch and the ability to convert into a fully lie-flat bed.

authorName: Review: Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo Business Class
content: Matthew Klint booked his ticket for the flight using British Airways Avios at a cost of 22,000 Avios and $38 in taxes, while a paid ticket could exceed 500 USD.

authorName: Review: Malaysia Airlines A330-900neo Business Class
content: Matthew Klint enjoyed an excellent flight from Kuala Lumpur to Bali on a brand new Malaysia Airlines Airbus A330-900neo in business class, highlighting the aircraft's comfort and excellent service onboard.

authorName: United Airlines Fundamentally Transforms MileagePlus With Credit Card–Driven Loyalty Strategy
content: MileagePlus is transitioning into a financial product focused on credit card loyalty rather than just a frequent flyer program, reflecting a significant shift in United Airlines' strategy.

authorName: United Airlines Fundamentally Transforms MileagePlus With Credit Card–Driven Loyalty Strategy
content: United Airlines is shifting its MileagePlus program to prioritize credit card adoption and spending over traditional loyalty based on flight activity.

authorName: United Airlines Fundamentally Transforms MileagePlus With Credit Card–Driven Loyalty Strategy
content: United Airlines is introducing a new debit card option that allows entry-level cardholders to earn enhanced mileage, requiring $10,000 in annual spending to unlock benefits.

authorName: United Airlines Fundamentally Transforms MileagePlus With Credit Card–Driven Loyalty Strategy
content: MileagePlus members who hold a United credit or debit card will receive automatic award discounts of at least 10%, making premium cabin awards more accessible for cardholders.

authorName: United Airlines Fundamentally Transforms MileagePlus With Credit Card–Driven Loyalty Strategy
content: United Airlines will eliminate mileage earning on Basic Economy fares for non-elite members unless they hold a United credit card, effective April 2, 2026.

authorName: United Airlines Fundamentally Transforms MileagePlus With Credit Card–Driven Loyalty Strategy
content: Starting April 2, 2026, MileagePlus members without a United co-branded credit card will earn substantially fewer redeemable miles on United flights compared to cardholders.

authorName: United Airlines Fundamentally Transforms MileagePlus With Credit Card–Driven Loyalty Strategy
content: United Airlines has announced significant changes to its MileagePlus loyalty program, shifting the focus towards rewarding credit card holders over non-cardholders.

authorName: American Airlines CEO Promises Big Changes But Can’t Explain Why Anyone Should Care—Employees And Investors Left In Limbo
content: American Airlines needs to establish a clear vision and narrative to unify its strategic decisions and communicate effectively with customers and employees about its future direction.

authorName: American Airlines CEO Promises Big Changes But Can’t Explain Why Anyone Should Care—Employees And Investors Left In Limbo
content: Robert Isom inherited a difficult situation from Doug Parker, who made poor decisions that burdened American Airlines with debt and stripped down its product offerings, complicating Isom's efforts to pivot towards a more premium experience.

authorName: American Airlines CEO Promises Big Changes But Can’t Explain Why Anyone Should Care—Employees And Investors Left In Limbo
content: American Airlines has the potential to improve operational reliability and customer service, but it faces challenges in executing these goals effectively, particularly in its Dallas and Charlotte hubs.

authorName: American Airlines CEO Promises Big Changes But Can’t Explain Why Anyone Should Care—Employees And Investors Left In Limbo
content: During a CNBC appearance, Robert Isom provided a list of initiatives but failed to convey a coherent vision for American Airlines, which is essential for guiding employees and reassuring customers.

authorName: American Airlines CEO Promises Big Changes But Can’t Explain Why Anyone Should Care—Employees And Investors Left In Limbo
content: American Airlines has struggled with financial performance, breaking even in 2025 while competitors Delta and United reported billion-dollar profits, leading to increased calls for CEO Robert Isom to resign.

authorName: American Airlines CEO Promises Big Changes But Can’t Explain Why Anyone Should Care—Employees And Investors Left In Limbo
content: Robert Isom has faced criticism for not articulating a clear vision for American Airlines, despite the airline making better decisions and improvements than in previous years under his leadership and that of his predecessor Doug Parker.

authorName: American Airlines CEO Promises Big Changes But Can’t Explain Why Anyone Should Care—Employees And Investors Left In Limbo
content: American Airlines is implementing significant changes, including new premium cabins and improved meals, but CEO Robert Isom has not clearly communicated a vision for the airline, leaving employees, customers, and investors uncertain about its future direction.

authorName: Delta’s $1.3 Billion Profit Sharing Day Was So Big Even IT Had DJs—Inside The Culture Keeping Employees Flying High
content: Delta Air Lines' profit sharing formula was preserved after a vote by pilots in 2015, ensuring that employees continue to benefit from the airline's profitability.

authorName: Delta’s $1.3 Billion Profit Sharing Day Was So Big Even IT Had DJs—Inside The Culture Keeping Employees Flying High
content: Delta Air Lines has a history of effectively managing employee relations and compensation, which has helped maintain its leadership position in the airline industry.

authorName: Delta’s $1.3 Billion Profit Sharing Day Was So Big Even IT Had DJs—Inside The Culture Keeping Employees Flying High
content: Delta Air Lines' strong corporate culture significantly contributes to its success by aligning employee incentives with customer service, creating a self-reinforcing cycle of profitability.

authorName: Delta’s $1.3 Billion Profit Sharing Day Was So Big Even IT Had DJs—Inside The Culture Keeping Employees Flying High
content: Delta Air Lines employees exhibit a higher level of job satisfaction and professionalism compared to employees at American Airlines, contributing to Delta's overall profitability.

authorName: Delta’s $1.3 Billion Profit Sharing Day Was So Big Even IT Had DJs—Inside The Culture Keeping Employees Flying High
content: Delta Air Lines fosters a positive workplace culture, as evidenced by celebrations at headquarters that included DJs, karaoke, and various treats, creating a festive atmosphere for employees on Profit Sharing Day.

authorName: Delta’s $1.3 Billion Profit Sharing Day Was So Big Even IT Had DJs—Inside The Culture Keeping Employees Flying High
content: Delta Air Lines' profit sharing exceeds the total amounts paid out by all other U.S. airlines combined, marking the ninth time since 2014 that Delta has paid over $1 billion in profit sharing.

authorName: Delta’s $1.3 Billion Profit Sharing Day Was So Big Even IT Had DJs—Inside The Culture Keeping Employees Flying High
content: Delta Air Lines celebrated Profit Sharing Day on February 13th by distributing $1.3 billion in bonuses to employees, which equates to 8.9% of their salaries or about a month's pay.

authorName: World’s Powerful Flight Attendant Heaps Effusive Praise On American Airlines CEO…
content: Sara Nelson, President of the Association of Flight Attendants, praised American Airlines CEO Robert Isom as the best leader for labor, despite ongoing tensions, following a new tentative contract that significantly raises wages for PSA flight attendants.

authorName: World’s Powerful Flight Attendant Heaps Effusive Praise On American Airlines CEO…
content: Sara Nelson, President of the Association of Flight Attendants, views Robert Isom's leadership as beneficial for labor because he has not imposed additional demands on flight attendants while providing significant pay raises.

authorName: World’s Powerful Flight Attendant Heaps Effusive Praise On American Airlines CEO…
content: Sara Nelson, President of the Association of Flight Attendants, criticized United Airlines for stalled negotiations after her team negotiated a deal for United flight attendants that was rejected.

authorName: World’s Powerful Flight Attendant Heaps Effusive Praise On American Airlines CEO…
content: Sara Nelson, President of the Association of Flight Attendants, praised American Airlines CEO Robert Isom while subtly criticizing United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby for his lack of direct involvement in negotiations for United flight attendants.

authorName: World’s Powerful Flight Attendant Heaps Effusive Praise On American Airlines CEO…
content: Sara Nelson, President of the Association of Flight Attendants, stated that American Airlines agreed to a shorter contract term, allowing workers to continue bargaining for their fair share of profits, which she believes is not the case with other airlines.

authorName: World’s Powerful Flight Attendant Heaps Effusive Praise On American Airlines CEO…
content: Nelson's praise for Isom contrasts with her criticism of United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby, whose flight attendants remain underpaid and without a new contract since 2019.

authorName: World’s Powerful Flight Attendant Heaps Effusive Praise On American Airlines CEO…
content: Sara Nelson, President of the Association of Flight Attendants, praised American Airlines CEO Robert Isom for his commitment to frontline employees after PSA flight attendants secured a tentative agreement for wage increases of up to 50%.

authorName: How to use and maximize Delta’s TakeOff 15 feature to save on award tickets
content: The TakeOff 15 benefit allows eligible Delta cobranded cardholders to save hundreds of dollars in SkyMiles annually, with no limit on how many times the benefit can be used.

authorName: How to use and maximize Delta’s TakeOff 15 feature to save on award tickets
content: Delta Air Lines will refund both miles and fees immediately after a customer cancels a ticket to take advantage of the TakeOff 15 discount, except for non-refundable basic economy award tickets.

authorName: How to use and maximize Delta’s TakeOff 15 feature to save on award tickets
content: Delta Air Lines customers can potentially save on previously booked flights by checking if the price has dropped with the TakeOff 15 feature and rebooking if necessary.

authorName: How to use and maximize Delta’s TakeOff 15 feature to save on award tickets
content: The TakeOff 15 discount applies only to Delta- and Delta Connection-operated flights and does not apply to Pay with Miles, Miles + Cash purchases, or upgrades purchased after ticketing.

authorName: How to use and maximize Delta’s TakeOff 15 feature to save on award tickets
content: To use the TakeOff 15 discount, Delta Air Lines customers must pay the taxes and fees with their eligible Delta cobranded American Express card when booking award flights.

authorName: How to use and maximize Delta’s TakeOff 15 feature to save on award tickets
content: Eligible Delta cobranded American Express cards for the TakeOff 15 discount include the Delta SkyMiles® Gold, Platinum, and Reserve cards, while the Delta SkyMiles® Blue card is not eligible.

authorName: How to use and maximize Delta’s TakeOff 15 feature to save on award tickets
content: Delta Air Lines offers a TakeOff 15 benefit that provides a 15% discount on all award flights for holders of eligible cobranded American Express cards.

content:
Initially, the A330 didn't sell that well because its range was only just over 5,500 nautical miles. That's useful for transatlantic routes between the US East Coast to central Europe but not much else in terms of long-distance flights. Hence the push for the smaller A330-200 with its range of 6,400 nautical miles, which DID become a very successful model for Airbus because its range meant it could fly many transoceanic routes.

Interestingly, the original A350 proposal from 2004 was essentially an A330 with more fuel capacity and new, much more efficient engines. But Qatar Airways wanted more out of the plane, and as such Airbus shelved that original proposal in favor of what became the A350XWB, a clean-sheet airplane design. But the much higher per-plane cost of the A350XWB was not acceptable to many airlines, though.

The A330neo came about because airlines still wanted a larger twin-engine widebody at lower cost. Given the much lower development cost (since it was essentially an A330 with new wings and engines, close to the original A350 proposal), Airbus went ahead with the project, securing an order from a small number of airlines (TAP Air Portugal being the first). But the A330-900neo became an unexpected success, mostly because of its 7,200 nautical mile range, which made it ideal as a 777-200ER successor. This is why A330-900neo sales have been surprisingly good.

content:
Thank you for another great video Ella 🤗
I'm also prioritising travel (again hehe), this year (and also a former travel agent 🤗). Last year my bf and I travelled business class (for the first time as well) part of the way to Europe - our hack has been to fly low cost carrier to Asia from Aus, then get our return full service airline tickets out of a big city like BKK or KUL.

We checked for an upgrade like 2 days before on our legs from Bangkok and found a ridiculous upgrade price on one of the legs, that was too good to pass up! We flew with Qatar and it was a dream!

May not be the most streamlined option (and not sure if you can upgrade like that with points) for some but we'll be travelling back to Europe through Asia again this year and found it one of the best value ways 😀

content:
Hokkaido has some of the best skiing on the planet and the prices are ridiculously lower than for example Aspen or Jackson Hole to the point that a return ticket to Japan and all other expenses included for a whole week of skiing, accommodation, food, transport and activities in Japan still can save you 1000 bucks in comparison or so. Especially now when the Yen is so cheap and the Euro is running away from the Dollar. I think the demand will shift from European skiing to Japanese skiing. 

Sapporo is also an up and coming tourism city in Japan after Tokyo, Kyoto and Osaka is so severely overcrowded with tourists due to the record low Yen that many complain it doesn’t have that Japanese magic anymore because you see more tourists than Japanese people in most areas. So many choose Sapporo instead for the authentic Japanese experience people say is like visiting Japan in the 1990s but in 2026!

So with this in mind, I think demand can be ramped up. Especially during winter.

content:
One of my pet hates-security at the gates, rather than the more efficient centralized system. Gates are always chaotic because of this, because less time is allowed between opening the gate and closing it. 
Malaysian airlines "hard product" can sometimes be a bit sketchy. My lie-flat seat on a long haul from KLIA to LHR didn't work. [of course I did not discover this until we were airborne].  They [the cabin staff] spent 15 minutes getting their hands dirty successfully fixing it. In addition, I found a few thousand extra flier points on my account as compensation. The meals and service was superb, and the captain [they had two] came around for a chat. 
In terms of 'soft product" the people, were friendly and very hard-working. From this aspect, Malaysian Airlines is "top tier" in my book, and very reasonably priced. 
In fact, before I had a flight  on Singapore airlines, and it was an inferior product.

content:
Malaysia Airlines has always have the best hospitality, friendly crew, hardworking, passionate about their job. But sadly they are limited to what they can afford to give you the best service imaginable, simply put, not that much capital unlike their competitors like Singapore, Qatar and so on. Just imagine if they have the capital like Qatar Airways did + the hardworking and very nice crew members that they already excel at, it would be an airline that is unbeatable. I've flown with those airlines with big capital like the Middle east carriers, The European ones, even the Japanese, most of the time the crew, I can say you can see that they're friendly, but not that friendly.

You get what I mean? It's like you can tell that they are the way they are because of their job, not necessarily because it's who they are as a person, with the exception of the Japanese carriers. So, given of what they had and the effort they tried to put for the passengers' best experience I think they did a great job. Sure you can argue that other airlines have a much better seats, meal, entertainment, perks and so on but that comes back to my main point. Money. They don't have that. And if only they had it. I can guarantee you it's gonna be a different quality of Malaysia Airlines that you would see. (by the way I'm not talking to this channel but rather just sharing my opinion to anyone who read this comment)

content: From what I've understood. Weight only plays a role in fuel consumption upon take off; once at cruising altitude the fuel is just there to overcome drag. Airlines care about weight for the luggage handlers at the airports. You still want to have the plane as light as you can get away with as that's extra fuel getting it up each flight. I've flown 17 hour flights from the middle east to Auckland. If I could fly direct from Europe and add a few more hours to the flight, that's better than killing 6 hours at a layover, going through security again etc. Usually we arrive during the day in Auckland. Last time it was 3am and it really sucked. Arriving in the afternoon is most ideal, as you can use the day light to set your body clock, try to stay awake until sun down, then go have the mega crash and wake up at some time closer to normal time. It takes a good week to get in the same sleep /wake cycle as destination locals.

content:
We recently flew from BHX to PMI (Well should have). The aircraft arrived on-time inbound but ended up with a tech fault. Within about 30 minutes of being informed, we received an email and text for a self-service platform, our only complaint was it tried to push us to just accept a refund. But we selected new flights, due to a lack of flights from BHX to PMI, we selected LGW to PMI, it then let us select a hotel, but most were some distance from LGW, so we decided to decline and claim our own hotel. We travelled down to Gatwick, claimed the train, hotel easyJet paid within 4 weeks. 

Very impressed - compared to some of the issues we have had with "Full Service" who do not use technology. 

I can imagine easyJet would have been a challenge if we were not okay with technology.

I recently did my first low cost pair of flights for similar reasons (no direct full service flights to my local airport) - out on Jet2 from Manchesterford.  Sad news, the new T2 is dire, rammed full, can't cope with the volume of passengers, boiling hot and picky security.  Other than that, with prepaid extras, it was quite pleasant - we booked extra legroom seats, and the flight was less than half full, so we even got a blocked middle seat!.  Flying back was with Ryanair, and how badly "joined together" everything was became aparent - due to the b-word passport stuff, they sent an email day before we flew back asking us to come and hour earlier.  Which we did.  but they didn't tell the check-in staff this, so baggage drop didn't open till the usual time & with new self-service baggage drop that worked a really odd way, with every single person needed help. we dropped bags the second it opened, dashed through the airport, and with the b-word biometric checks, arrived at the gate at boardingnotboarding started.  we had time to buy a bottle of pop each, and that was it!  I wonder if this continues if the airport will actually lose out with no one having time to buy food / duty free

content:
I recently priced up a trip to Europe with BA and EasyJet. As I wanted an overhead locker bag, this basically meant there was very little difference in fare which I think is interesting and shows how BA's cost cutting has enabled them to get to EasyJet's pricing points. Because I have status with BA, I chose BA as I'll get a much better airport experience (and not have to trek out to Luton, with the additional expense of the train).

I disagree with your comments about the EES - when I travel to Dubai, Singapore and many other countries, I am through the border in minutes via an e-Gate without being in a political union with those countries. The UK border is also another good example - we let a big chunk of trusted travellers through using e-Gates without being in Unions with them. Norway and Switzerland passport holders have long been able to use EU lanes at EU airports too. So Brexit isn't the issue, it's the completely terrible way the EU has implemented the EES - which hopefully will get sorted out. And TBH, the UK government probably should have negotiated an opt out to the EES for the UK or a guarantee we can use EU lanes like Switzerland and Norway. But remember, if you're complaining about a long queue in a non EU lane, those very same British people would be in the EU lane had Brexit not happened (esp at places like Alicante where I would guess a big chunk of the non EU traffic is to the UK), so the situation wouldn't really be any different! Indeed at some European airports it's been a blessing to be able to use the shorter non EU lane.
